exemplar.title: How do combustion chambers and their designs differ between various engines, impacting the resulting torque and horsepower output?

detailedExemplarData.title: How do combustion chambers and their designs differ between various engines, impacting the resulting torque and horsepower output?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Methodology uses a varied range of secondary sources and logical derivations
Research question is explicitly stated and maintained throughout
weaknesses: Key assumptions (e.g., neglecting friction, constant air density) are not fully justified
Research question is still broad, covering multiple chamber types and performance variables
No attempt to validate the theoretical model experimentally
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work solidly in the 3â4 band: the focus and methodology are adequate and coherent but lack full justification and experimental validation. A mid-band score of 3 reflects both the clear purpose and the remaining gaps in justification and focus.

strengths: Sound explanation of four-stroke cycles and pressureâvolume work
Logical derivation and application of thermodynamic equations to engine data
Mostly accurate use of specialist terminology
weaknesses: Incorrect representation of the Arrhenius relation and occasional notation errors
Missing units in tables leading to potential ambiguity
Simplified treatment of combustion intermediates and efficiency factors
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates good subject knowledge and generally correct terminology, but occasional inaccuracies, simplifications, and inconsistent notation prevent an upper-band score. A mid-band mark of 3 is therefore appropriate.

strengths: Coherent analysis linking torque/RPM data to combustion-chamber design
Well-structured argument progressing from theory through data to conclusion
Dedicated section on limitations and extensions shows genuine critical reflection
weaknesses: No statistical or uncertainty analysis to support quantitative claims
Reasoning occasionally relies on unsupported general statements
Evaluation remains qualitative and is not consistently integrated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis and argument clearly address the research question, and the student identifies limitations, placing the work in the 4â6 band. However, the absence of quantitative uncertainty treatment and fully integrated evaluation keeps it below the top of the band. A score of 5 reflects solid critical thinking with room for deeper analysis.

strengths: Appropriate overall structure following EE conventions (title page, TOC, headings)
Tables and figures are present and labelled
weaknesses: Inconsistent layoutâlow-resolution images, truncated headings, uneven spacing
Captions embedded in paragraphs rather than placed consistently
Minor formatting flaws in table borders and text continuity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reportâs structure supports understanding, but a number of layout and formatting inconsistencies hinder clarity. These characteristics align with the 1â2 band for Presentation, meriting a 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: Scientific terminology (BDC, TDC, swept volume, AFR, thermal efficiency) is used correctly in most cases, and mathematical notation is mostly consistent.  Occasional lapses (confusing proportionality in Arrhenius equation, units not always shown) show that command is not fully consistent, so a safe mid-band mark is appropriate.

An argument is developed that diesel chambers favour torque, rotaries favour high-RPM power, etc.  The discussion follows a logical structure from background to derivations, data, interpretation and conclusion.  Nonetheless, reasoning sometimes relies on general statements (âlimitless RPMâ, âwill break the engineâ) without quantitative backing, and alternative explanations are not explored.  Thus the argument is well-developed but not highly sophisticated.

A logical, predominantly secondary-data method is described: derive equations from thermodynamic principles, gather manufacturer specifications, and calculate torque/horsepower.  Sources are varied and mostly appropriate; tables show the data collected.  Nevertheless, assumptions (e.g. neglecting friction, constant air density) are not fully justified and no attempt is made to validate the model experimentally, so the methodology is âmostly completeâ rather than fully appropriate.  This positions it comfortably within the 3-4 band.

The student demonstrates good grasp of internal-combustion physics, correctly outlining four-stroke cycles, pressureâvolume work and links to torque.  Equations are derived logically and applied to real engine data.  Some inaccuracies (mis-statement of Arrhenius relation; simplified constant densities) and lack of depth in thermodynamic efficiency prevent the work from reaching âexcellentâ, but overall knowledge is sound and generally well applied.

There is a discrete âLimitations and Extensionsâ section identifying scope restrictions, data-quality issues and modelling assumptions, showing genuine reflection.  However, evaluation is largely qualitative and not integrated throughout the body of the essay; the impact of limitations on conclusions is only briefly noted.  This aligns with âgood evaluation with some critical analysisâ in the 4-6 band, bottom half.

The student states a clear, recognisable focus on the comparative thermodynamic behaviour of different combustion-chamber geometries. The introduction gives sufficient contextual background and signposts the purpose of linking chamber design to torque/horse-power.  Terminology such as âswept volumeâ, âstrokeâ and âair-fuel ratioâ is employed accurately enough for the reader to understand what is being investigated.  Minor ambiguity remains (e.g. whether the study is limited to theoretical output or real-world engines), so the communication is solid rather than exemplary, justifying the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Visual elements (three small figures and three data tables) are present and labelled, but image resolution is low and some tables overflow margins.  The layout is functional yet exhibits inconsistencies (uneven spacing, truncated table headings).  These shortcomings fit the description of a âbasic layout with some inconsistenciesâ, meriting a 1-2 band mark, here the upper value 2.

The research question is explicitly presented (âHow do combustion chambers â¦ impact torque and horsepower?â) and is linked throughout the essay; however it is still quite broad, combining three chamber types, two fuels and two performance variables.  At times the discussion drifts into engine applications rather than remaining tightly focused on chamber thermodynamics.  Hence the question is clear but only partially focused, meriting the mid-band score.

The student analyses data from fifteen engines, compares torque/RPM relationships, and links results to design variables and fuel type.  Calculations are shown and observations draw on the numerical outcomes.  While the analysis is coherent, it remains largely descriptive of patterns; statistical treatment, uncertainty or sensitivity analysis is absent.  This reflects âadequate to goodâ analysis squarely inside the 4-6 band, here awarded 5.

The essay follows EE conventions: title page with word count, clear table of contents, numbered headings, tables and figures, and a works-cited list.  Minor formatting flaws (some captions embedded in text, inconsistent table borders) exist but do not hinder comprehension.  Overall structure is clear and appropriate, supporting effective communication; hence top band is justified but lower score within it.

annotations.comment: The word count is provided and falls within the 4000-word limit, fulfilling the formatting requirement.
The research question is clearly stated but splits across lines, which slightly disrupts readability. Consider rephrasing into a single concise sentence to maintain focus.
Thermal efficiency \(\eta\) appears without stated assumptions or source range. Indicate how values were chosen and discuss typical efficiency variations.
Assuming constant air density of 1.225 kg/mÂ³ at SATP oversimplifies variations with temperature and pressure. Justify or discuss the impact of this approximation on results.
Tables extend beyond page margins and row borders are inconsistent. Adjust table formatting and sizing to fit within margins and standardize borders.
Units for torque (e.g., NÂ·m) and horsepower are missing from table headings, which may lead to misinterpretation of numerical values.
Citation entries vary in style and completeness. Standardize formatting (e.g., consistent use of accessed dates, punctuation) to adhere to academic conventions.
The title succinctly conveys the focus on thermodynamic analysis and engine efficiency, guiding reader expectations effectively.
An empty row in the table of contents appears without content. Remove or complete this entry to maintain structural clarity.

The introduction provides historical context and motivation but lacks immediate connection to thermodynamic principles. Introduce key definitions earlier to ground the reader in the studyâs physics basis.

The representation of the Arrhenius relation as $$T \propto \frac{1}{F}$$ is incorrect. Clarify and use the correct form $$k = A e^{-E_a/(RT)}$$ to accurately describe temperature dependence of reaction rates.
The description of spark ignition implies complete conversion of gasoline into COâ; consider mentioning intermediate species and incomplete combustion to reflect real processes.
The timing of diesel injection is oversimplified as occurring during the power stroke. Clarify that injection timing is near TDC to optimize combustion rather than spanning the full stroke.
Figure 3âs caption is embedded within the paragraph, disrupting flow. Position captions immediately below figures and separate from body text for consistency.
This paragraph ends abruptly, cutting off the definition of torque. Ensure sentences are complete to convey full meaning.
The methodological section includes personal motivation statements. Focus this section on procedural details to maintain academic tone, moving reflections to the conclusion or reflection section.
The constant 7120 in the horsepower equation lacks derivation or reference. Provide justification or citation to clarify its origin.
The relation $$T = \frac{F \cdot R_{crank}}{2} = \frac{F \cdot S_{length}}{4}$$ is stated without derivation. Include a brief derivation to demonstrate understanding of geometry involved.
The variable \(B\) is labeled as 'B ore length', which may confuse readers. Standardize terminology to 'Bore length' and ensure consistent notation throughout.
The consolidated horsepower formula is dense. Consider breaking it into intermediary steps with explanations to improve reader comprehension.
This sentence is incomplete, ending with 'assumed that no'. Complete or remove to avoid confusion in methodology description.
Observations describe trends verbally but lack quantitative analysis of ratios and statistical significance. Include numerical comparisons to strengthen argument.
The hyperbolic claim that rotaries can 'spin indefinitely' is unrealistic. Replace with more precise language regarding mechanical limits and operational RPM ranges.
The term 'fallacies' in the context of environmental impacts is misused. Consider replacing with 'aspects' or 'factors' to accurately convey intended meaning.
Acknowledgement of data quality issues is good, but the analysis lacks any error or uncertainty estimates. Incorporate margins of error to evaluate confidence in results.
